features.name: Put the cache on screen
features.desc: Three caches decide whether my code even runs — fetch, full route, router — and every one of them is invisible. A cache I can't see isn't a feature, it's a bug with a delay on it. Show it in dev: what's cached, why, and the one button to bust it. That single panel closes half your GitHub issues, because most of them are someone staring at stale output with no idea which layer lied.

features.name: One rendering story, in one sentence
features.desc: 1,000 songs in your pocket. That's a product. SSR, SSG, ISR, RSC, use client, use server is a manual. The framework isn't too complex — the explanation is, and the explanation is what people actually use. If a junior can't repeat what your framework does after one read, the power doesn't matter because nobody reaches it. Pick one mental model, lead every doc with it, and let the rest be depth you opt into.

features.name: Own the whole path, or stop pretending
features.desc: You're insanely good from my component to the edge, and then you hand me a dozen ways to fetch, cache, and deploy and call it flexibility. Flexibility is what you say when you haven't decided. Pick the one path you'd stake your name on and make it the road everyone defaults to. Half-owning the experience is exactly the seam where it leaks, and the leaks are what people remember.

more.name: Type the route, not the folder tree
more.desc: Filesystem routing is magic until you're three folders deep hunting which layout owns a URL. Give me typed, autocompletable routes I can click through from the editor, and a real error when a link points nowhere. The convention is fine; the invisibility is the tax.

more.name: One blessed way to fetch data
more.desc: Right now it's fetch in a server component, or a server action, or a route handler, or a client library — four answers to one question. Pick the one you'd teach a junior on day one and make every doc lead with it. The others can stay as depth, not as a coin flip on every screen.

more.name: An error overlay that names the fix
more.desc: When something breaks, don't hand me a hydration stack trace and wish me luck. Tell me the one boundary to move, the one prop that changed, the one line to delete — and why. The error you explain is the support ticket you never get.

more.name: Make 'use client' the rare case
more.desc: It's become a reflex — people paste it at the top of a file to make a red error go away, and now half the app ships to the browser that never needed to. Default to the correct boundary so the directive is something you reach for on purpose, maybe twice a project, not something you sprinkle to survive.

more.name: One config file, fully commented
more.desc: next.config has quietly become a junk drawer of flags, experimental toggles, and plugin wrappers nobody remembers turning on. Collapse it to one file where every option is explained inline and the defaults are the ones you'd actually recommend. Config you can read is config you can trust.

more.name: A clean way out, not a trap door
more.desc: When the magic fights someone — and eventually it fights everyone — give them a way to drop down a level that doesn't feel like leaving the framework forever. An honest escape hatch keeps people in. A locked door is how you lose the senior who hits the wall once and never forgets it.

more.name: Tell me what the build actually did
more.desc: After a build I want one screen, not a wall of logs: every route, its size, static or dynamic, and what got cached. Make the cost of each page obvious at a glance and people fix the fat route on their own. Silence is how 4MB bundles ship.

killed: the Pages Router (you have two — pick one)
three ways to load a font
a plugin API for the plugin API
config for things that deserve one right default

verdict.quote: You built the best way to ship React, then kept building more ways to ship React. Two routers. Five caches. A server component and a client component with a footgun on every line between them. When I came back to Apple I cut three hundred and fifty products down to ten, because focus isn't saying yes to the good idea — it's saying no to the thousand other good ideas. You have the opposite reflex. The power is real, and nobody can hold it in their head. Pick one router. Put the cache on screen. Tell one rendering story a kid gets on the first read. You don't need more surface. You need the nerve to delete half of it.
